primary_diagnosis.name: Brugada syndrome (fever-triggered), presenting as aborted sudden cardiac arrest with nocturnal syncope

pathophysiology_summary: Brugada syndrome is an inherited cardiac sodium-channelopathy (classically loss-of-function SCN5A variants) that predisposes to polymorphic ventricular tachycardia and ventricular fibrillation, typically at rest or during sleep when vagal tone is high. Fever is a well-recognised precipitant: elevated temperature further impairs sodium-channel kinetics, unmasking the type-1 coved ST-segment elevation in the right precordial leads (V1–V3) and provoking malignant arrhythmia. This patient's nocturnal loss of consciousness with agonal (gasping) respirations is the hallmark of a self-terminating ventricular arrhythmia — an aborted sudden cardiac arrest — rather than a simple faint. The structurally normal heart, the supine/asleep timing, the febrile trigger, and a first-degree relative with premature sudden death in sleep together point strongly to an inherited primary arrhythmia syndrome. The immediate risk is recurrence of a lethal arrhythmia while the fever persists, which is why continuous ECG monitoring and definitive electrophysiological assessment cannot wait until morning.

differential.diagnosis: Syncope of undetermined cause (transient loss of consciousness)
differential.evidence_basis: Witnessed transient loss of consciousness with spontaneous recovery — the syndrome must be risk-stratified before a cause is assumed.
Nocturnal collapse with noisy breathing and post-event confusion can mimic a seizure.
Gasping/snoring respirations during sleep raise OSA — but a single acute collapse is not typical.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI Doctor should treat this as high-risk syncope until proven otherwise, not default to a benign faint.

differential.diagnosis: Cardiac (arrhythmic) syncope / aborted sudden cardiac arrest
differential.evidence_basis: Supine/asleep onset, agonal breathing, and a first-degree relative dead suddenly in sleep at 30 are classic high-risk features of an arrhythmic cause.
Young structurally-normal-heart patient, febrile trigger, and familial premature sudden death point to Brugada / long-QT / CPVT-type channelopathy.
No sustained tonic-clonic activity, no tongue-biting, no incontinence, and rapid recovery without a prolonged post-ictal phase argue against a primary seizure.
Vasovagal syncope essentially does not occur while supine and asleep and would not produce agonal breathing.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The presence of supine/asleep syncope + agonal breathing + familial sudden death should move the AI Doctor decisively toward a dangerous cardiac cause and away from benign mimics.

differential.evidence_basis: A 12-lead ECG (with high right precordial leads) showing coved type-1 ST elevation in V1–V3 during fever is diagnostic — but requires hospital ECG the AI Doctor cannot obtain.
Also causes nocturnal/rest arrhythmic syncope with familial sudden death; distinguished on the ECG QT interval.
Inherited channelopathy with exertional/emotional arrhythmia — less typical for a sleep event but part of the SCD work-up.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The definitive test is a 12-lead ECG under continuous monitoring in hospital; the AI Doctor's correct move is to escalate for that, not to attempt a remote diagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: Fever-triggered malignant ventricular arrhythmia on a background inherited channelopathy (Brugada syndrome) — aborted sudden cardiac arrest
differential.evidence_basis: Whole constellation: nocturnal/supine syncope, agonal breathing, febrile trigger, familial premature sudden death, structurally normal heart. Confirmation is a hospital ECG/EP assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A pass does not require naming Brugada; it requires recognising an aborted cardiac arrest / high-risk arrhythmic syncope and escalating to emergency continuous ECG monitoring.

red_flags.description: Syncope occurring while supine and asleep (loss of consciousness at rest, without postural or situational trigger)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Syncope at rest/supine is a hallmark of an arrhythmic (cardiac) cause and effectively excludes benign vasovagal syncope.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling as a simple faint leads to discharge home and risks a fatal recurrent arrhythmia overnight.

red_flags.description: Agonal (gasping) respirations during the episode — marker of a self-terminating cardiac arrest rather than a faint
red_flags.clinical_significance: Agonal breathing accompanies cerebral hypoperfusion from a malignant arrhythmia; it signifies an aborted sudden cardiac arrest.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise an aborted arrest as an emergency delays monitoring and defibrillation capability.

red_flags.description: Family history of sudden unexplained death in a young first-degree relative (brother died in sleep at age 30)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Premature sudden death in a first-degree relative is a strong marker of an inherited primary arrhythmia syndrome and independently raises the patient's risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the familial red flag anchors the assessment on a benign cause and misses an inherited channelopathy.

red_flags.description: Active febrile illness at the time of the arrhythmic event
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever is a recognised trigger that unmasks type-1 Brugada ECG changes and provokes ventricular arrhythmia; while the fever persists, recurrence risk is high.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not appreciating fever as an ongoing arrhythmic trigger under-estimates the imminent recurrence risk and the need for aggressive antipyresis plus monitoring.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a witnessed collapse with quick recovery as an uncomplicated vasovagal faint and reassuring the patient home — ignoring that supine/asleep syncope is a red flag for an arrhythmic cause.

Labelling the event a nocturnal seizure because of noisy breathing and brief confusion, and referring to neurology non-urgently — when the absence of tonic-clonic activity, tongue-biting and prolonged post-ictal state argues against seizure and for cardiac syncope.
Attributing the gasping to obstructive sleep apnoea and arranging an outpatient sleep study, missing that a single acute collapse with family history of sudden death is not OSA.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a normal exam and normal vitals
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured that the patient now feels fine, looks well and has normal vitals and a structurally normal heart — a normal resting state is expected between arrhythmic events and does not lower the risk.
Advising the patient to see their GP or attend in the morning, when the febrile trigger makes a recurrent lethal arrhythmia possible within hours and overnight.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG with high right precordial leads (the diagnostic test — coved type-1 ST elevation in V1–V3)
Continuous cardiac telemetry to capture a recurrent arrhythmia
Cardiac auscultation and confirmation of a structurally normal heart
Measured blood pressure, heart rate and rhythm
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance now for continuous ECG monitoring and transfer to an emergency department; do not allow the patient to go back to sleep or wait until morning. Coach the partner to be ready to start CPR and call back immediately if the patient collapses or stops breathing normally.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The AI Doctor must not attempt to confirm or exclude a specific channelopathy remotely, and must not be reassured by a benign mimic. The dangerous error is UNDER-action — treating this as a faint, seizure or sleep-apnoea event and not escalating for emergency ECG monitoring.

guidelines_referenced.reference: HRS/EHRA/APHRS Expert Consensus Statement on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Fever should be treated promptly and aggressively in Brugada syndrome, and fever-provoked arrhythmic syncope warrants urgent cardiac monitoring and electrophysiology assessment.

guidelines_referenced.reference: CSANZ Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Familial Sudden Cardiac Death syndromes
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Unexplained syncope at rest with a family history of premature sudden death mandates cardiology assessment and consideration of an inherited arrhythmia syndrome.
